title: Calcolatore di Stipendio
description: Converti lo stipendio tra orario, giornaliero, mensile e annuale. Calcola il netto dalla RAL con simulazione IRPEF italiana.

faq.title: Domande Frequenti
faq.q1: Come converto uno stipendio orario in annuale?
faq.a1: Stipendio annuale = Tariffa oraria × Ore settimanali × Settimane lavorative/anno. Per full-time standard (40 ore/settimana, 52 settimane, meno ~2 settimane di ferie = 50 settimane): €15/ora × 40 × 50 = €30.000/anno. O il metodo standard: €15 × 2.080 ore (40h × 52 settimane) = €31.200 lordi.
faq.a2: La RAL è il totale delle retribuzioni lorde percepite in un anno: stipendio base + tredicesima + eventuali quattordicesima, premi, indennità. È l'importo indicato nei contratti e nelle offerte di lavoro. Dal RAL si sottraggono contributi INPS del dipendente (~9,19%) e IRPEF per ottenere il netto effettivamente ricevuto.
faq.a3: L'IRPEF è progressiva: fino a €28.000 → 23%, €28.001-50.000 → 35%, oltre €50.000 → 43%. Sul reddito da lavoro si applicano detrazioni per lavoro dipendente (decrescenti con il reddito, max €1.880/anno per redditi fino a €15.000). Addizionali regionali e comunali aggiungono circa 1,5-2,5%. Il calcolo preciso richiede il modello 730.
faq.q4: Qual è la differenza tra RAL e busta paga mensile?
faq.a4: La busta paga mensile = RAL / 13 (o 14 per contratti con quattordicesima), al netto dei contributi INPS e IRPEF. Esempio: RAL €30.000. Contributi INPS dipendente: ~€2.757. Reddito imponibile IRPEF: ~€27.243. IRPEF stimata: ~€5.400. Netto annuo: ~€21.843. Netto mensile: ~€1.680 (diviso 13).
faq.q5: Cosa sono i contributi INPS del lavoratore?
faq.a5: I contributi INPS del lavoratore dipendente sono circa il 9,19% della retribuzione lorda (aliquota standard per la maggior parte dei lavoratori). Contribuiscono al calcolo della pensione futura. Il datore di lavoro paga ulteriori contributi (circa 28-32%) che non compaiono in busta paga ma rappresentano il costo totale del lavoro.
faq.q6: Cosa è il cuneo fiscale in Italia?
faq.a6: Il cuneo fiscale è la differenza tra il costo totale del lavoratore per l'azienda e il netto che riceve il dipendente. In Italia è circa il 45-47% — tra i più alti in Europa. Esempio: costo azienda €40.000 → netto dipendente ~€22.000. La differenza (~€18.000) va in contributi e imposte. Il governo ha introdotto parziali riduzioni del cuneo negli ultimi anni.
faq.q7: Come si calcola il TFR (Trattamento di Fine Rapporto)?
faq.a7: Il TFR matura al ritmo di circa 1/13,5 della retribuzione annua lorda. Formula: TFR annuo = RAL / 13,5. Per RAL €30.000: TFR ≈ €2.222/anno. Si rivaluta dell'1,5% fisso + 0,75% dell'inflazione ISTAT annualmente. Viene erogato alla fine del rapporto di lavoro (o versato al fondo pensione se il dipendente lo sceglie).
faq.q8: Come si negozia lo stipendio in Italia?
faq.a8: In Italia la retribuzione minima è fissata dai CCNL (Contratti Collettivi Nazionali di Lavoro) per categoria. Nelle trattative: ricerca le tabelle retributive del tuo CCNL, considera benefit extra come buoni pasto, auto aziendale, welfare aziendale (spesso più convenienti del lordo per entrambe le parti). Il welfare aziendale (fino a €3.000/anno) è esente da contributi e imposte.

content.whatIs:
A salary calculator converts compensation between any two common pay period formats — hourly to annual, annual to hourly — and instantly calculates the equivalent pay across every standard interval: daily, weekly, biweekly, monthly, and yearly. Enter your rate in any format with your work schedule, and every other equivalent figure appears in one calculation.

The need for salary-to-hourly conversion is nearly universal. Job seekers compare offers expressed in different formats — a salaried offer of $68,000 versus a contract role at $38/hour. Freelancers must translate client hourly budgets into annual income projections to evaluate financial viability. Employees negotiating raises need to know the hourly equivalent of their current salary to benchmark against market rates. Part-time workers calculating eligibility for tax credits or government benefits often need to express irregular income as annual figures. This calculator handles all of these scenarios with a single pair of inputs.

The calculator is built on the standard U.S. payroll convention: 40 hours per week, 52 weeks per year for the annual figure, and 261 working days per year for the daily rate. These defaults reflect a standard full-time schedule and match the conventions used by most HR systems and salary benchmarking databases. The hours and weeks fields are fully adjustable for non-standard schedules.
content.formula:
Standard conversion formulas:

Annual = Hourly × Hours/Week × Weeks/Year
Hourly = Annual ÷ (Hours/Week × Weeks/Year)

From Annual:
  Monthly  = Annual ÷ 12
  Biweekly = Annual ÷ 26   (26 pay periods per year)
  Weekly   = Annual ÷ 52
  Daily    = Annual ÷ 261  (standard U.S. working days)

Default assumptions:
  Full-time: 40 hours/week, 52 weeks/year
  Working days/year: 261 (52 × 5 = 260, rounded to 261)

Overtime rate = Hourly × 1.5 (for hours > 40/week, FLSA standard)
content.example:
Example 1 — Standard full-time worker:
$28/hour, 40 hours/week, 52 weeks/year.
Annual: $28 × 40 × 52 = $58,240
Monthly: $58,240 ÷ 12 = $4,853.33
Biweekly: $58,240 ÷ 26 = $2,240
Weekly: $58,240 ÷ 52 = $1,120
Daily: $58,240 ÷ 261 = $223.14

Example 2 — Salaried professional:
$95,000/year salary.
Hourly equivalent (40h/52w): $95,000 ÷ 2,080 = $45.67/hour
Monthly: $7,916.67
Biweekly paycheck: $3,653.85
Daily rate: $364.37

Example 3 — Part-time worker:
$18/hour, 25 hours/week, 48 weeks/year (takes 4 weeks unpaid).
Annual: $18 × 25 × 48 = $21,600
Monthly average: $1,800
Biweekly: $830.77
content.howItWorks_1: Select your salary type — hourly or annual. Enter your rate: if hourly, enter your hourly wage; if annual, enter your total gross annual salary. Adjust hours per week if your schedule differs from the standard 40 hours (common for part-time, 37.5-hour, or compressed schedules). Adjust weeks per year if you take unpaid time off or work seasonally.
content.howItWorks_2: Click Calculate. All equivalent pay period amounts are computed simultaneously using the standard conversion formulas. The results represent gross earnings before any tax, insurance, or retirement deductions.
content.howItWorks_3: Compare the full suite of figures to evaluate job offers, budget personal finances, or set freelance rates. The hourly equivalent of an annual salary is particularly useful when benchmarking against hourly rate data from salary surveys, Glassdoor, or industry reports. The monthly figure maps directly to monthly budgeting and expense planning.
content.whyItMatters:
Salary literacy — understanding what your compensation means across different time frames — is a foundational financial skill that affects both career decisions and personal budgeting. Many workers accept salary offers without fully translating them into practical terms. A biweekly paycheck of $2,307 sounds different from an annual salary of $60,000 — even though they are identical — and different again from $28.85/hour. The format in which compensation is presented affects how people evaluate and compare it.

When evaluating job offers, the ability to convert all offers to a common format (usually hourly or annual) is essential for accurate comparison. A $55,000 annual offer at a company with generous benefits, 4 weeks vacation, and a 5% 401(k) match may be more valuable than a $65,000 offer with 2 weeks vacation, poor benefits, and no match. To compare these accurately, you need to monetize the benefits gap — the vacation difference alone (2 extra weeks at $26.44/hour = $2,115), plus the 401(k) match difference ($2,750), closes a significant portion of the $10,000 nominal gap.

For freelancers and contractors, the salary-to-hourly conversion is critical for sustainable rate-setting. A common mistake among new freelancers is setting rates equivalent to their former salaried hourly rate without accounting for self-employment taxes (15.3% vs. 7.65%), lost benefits (health insurance: $400–800/month self-pay), unbillable time (client acquisition, administration, sick days), and income variability. The rule of thumb is that freelancers need to earn 1.5–2× their equivalent salaried hourly rate to achieve the same net income and benefits.
content.limitations:
This calculator shows gross salary figures before all taxes and deductions. Net take-home pay can be substantially lower — typically 65–75% of gross for middle-income U.S. earners after federal income tax, Social Security, Medicare, state taxes, and common benefit deductions. The exact net amount depends on your tax filing status, deductions, state of residence, and benefit elections. For accurate net pay calculation, use a payroll calculator that applies your specific tax parameters.

The daily rate calculation uses 261 working days, which is a standard payroll convention but slightly different from actual working days in a given year. In 2024, the actual number of weekdays is 262. Federal holidays (10 per year) and company-specific holidays reduce available working days further — from 252 to 261 depending on the organization. For consulting day rate calculations, using 250 working days (subtracting 10 federal holidays) may be more appropriate depending on contract terms.

This calculator does not account for overtime, bonuses, commissions, equity compensation, or non-salary benefits. For many jobs — particularly in sales, finance, and technology — these components can substantially exceed base salary. A financial advisor earning $80,000 base with $40,000 annual bonus has a total compensation of $120,000, not $80,000. Tech employees with significant equity grants may have total compensation 2–3× their base salary. Always evaluate total compensation, not just base salary, when comparing offers.
content.tips_1: When comparing job offers, convert everything to the same annual gross figure first, then add the monetary value of benefits. Health insurance, 401(k) match, PTO, and remote work flexibility have real dollar values. Employer-sponsored health insurance for a family plan averages $6,000–8,000 per year in employer contribution — a significant component of total compensation that disappears in contractor or self-employed roles.
content.tips_2: Use the hourly rate to set a personal time value benchmark. If your hourly equivalent is $30/hour, tasks that cost less than $30 to outsource (laundry, grocery delivery, house cleaning) are worth paying for — your time has higher economic value. This framing helps prioritize time and reduces 'I should do this myself' thinking for tasks that cost you more in time than in money.
content.tips_3: For freelancers: set your rate by calculating your required annual income and dividing by your billable hours — not your total working hours. If you need $80,000/year and can realistically bill 1,200 hours per year (accounting for 40% unbillable overhead), your minimum viable rate is $80,000 ÷ 1,200 = $66.67/hour — before self-employment tax, benefits, and profit margin. Many freelancers dramatically underprice their services because they calculate off total hours rather than billable hours.
content.tips_4: Know your target salary range before any negotiation by researching 3–5 credible sources. Bureau of Labor Statistics OES data provides median and percentile salaries by occupation and geography. LinkedIn Salary, Glassdoor, and Levels.fyi provide self-reported data more current than government surveys. Present your research as factual benchmarking, not personal need: 'Based on BLS data for this role in this market, the 75th percentile is $78,000 — I am targeting that range' is far stronger than 'I need more money for my expenses.'
